channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.rockpapershotgun.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.rockpapershotgun.com

item.title: Valve roll out a more detailed timeline for Steam Controller orders, giving you a clearer picture of when you'll hear about your reservation
item.content:encoded:
"We want to manage expectations as much as we can"

If you've put in an order for a Steam Controller and are still awaiting its arrival, a new system Valve have rolled out can offer you an estimate of when you should hear from them about your order. While you'll only be given one of three very rough windows, it's at least something to go on if you're wondering where you're at in the queue.

"When we launched Steam Controller last month, we quickly saw that initial demand exceeded our expectations," Valve wrote in a news post about the more detailed timeline for reservations they've just deployed. "Switching to a reservation queue has (hopefully) cut down on the headaches on the customer side, and for us it's also been helpful as we plan ahead and try to get as many out as quickly as we are able.

"We have no plans to stop making Steam Controller. But as we look at the current demand compared to how many we know we can make by the end of the year, we want to manage expectations as much as we can with regards to when folks can expect to receive their order." So, heading over the Steam Controller page while logged in will now show folks awaiting their order a time window by which they can expect to receive an email about it. You'll see one of three timelines: by September 2026, by December 2026, and in 2027.

Valve have gone for these three in an effort to keep things simple, with those who end up with the 2027 window being reassured that "additional information on specific timing" will arrive further down the line. Those who've not yet ordered a controller, but decide to do so from this point forward will also be shown an estimated window - most likely to be 2027 right now since you'll be joining the back of the queue.

Once you finally get that elusive email about your reservation, you'll have 72 hours to go through with the order before Valve move on to the next person in line.

Here's James' Steam Controller review, if you've still not digested the pros and cons of it. Meanwhile, its siblings the Steam Machine and Steam Frame are set to go on sale this summer, following memory crisis-fuelled delays.



https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/valve-roll-out-a-more-detailed-timeline-for-steam-controller-orders-giving-you-a-clearer-picture-of-when-youll-hear-about-your-reservation
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Epic's plans for their big store revamp over the next year sound like they'll at long last meet the bare minimum
item.content:encoded:
The Epic Games Store is not very good. This is not a controversial thing to say, I'm fairly certain, as the lack of features available within it feels a bit odd also eight years into the thing existing. Sure, there's some free games every now and then (like Citizen Sleeper, right now), but there's a lot of basic things missing. But it seems much of that, including simple things like patch notes, will be coming within the next 12 months.

Over on the EpicGamesPC subreddit, a series of photos from a talk Epic held regarding the storefront's future shows off the kinds of things you can expect from it over the next year. A roadmap of sorts outlines what you can expect when, with smaller things like pre-registration for free-to-play games, patch notes, a private beta for the next version of the launcher, improved storefront navigation, and whatever the hell "Fortnite Chunked Installation" is (I'm assuming that it lets you download only certain parts of the battle royale) coming up first.

Epic Games Store roadmap for 2026 and more screenshots of launcher rebuild
by u/ImAnthlon in EpicGamesPC

To see this content please enable targeting cookies.

Speaking of that revamped storefront, a couple images of that were shown off, with some captions explaining that home pages will be personalised per user, highlighting games that match an individual's "interests and playstyle." Sounds pretty basic! Game pages will apparently be more detailed too, with again some tailoring done for each user to "show what's relevant to them based on where they are in the game."

In the up next section of the roadmap, there's also a mention of user written reviews, the absence of which is a commonly levied criticism against the storefront, alongside supposed discovery improvements, and the launch of the next version of the storefront. A look at user profiles was also shown off, which shows things like games a person owns, what they've been playing recently, achievements. Once again, literally nothing unique to be found here.

It is quite odd that Epic have been taking so long to add such basic elements of a digital storefront. But then, they did lay off over 1000 employees earlier this year, so, it doesn't really seem like they know how to run a team very well. At least Unreal Engine 6 will incorporate generative AI! I definitely feel good about that! Please, oh god please, read the sarcasm here.



https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/epics-plans-for-their-big-store-revamp-over-the-next-year-sound-like-theyll-at-long-last-meet-the-bare-minimum
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
